title: Romans 7
subtitle: The Law and Sin

sections.header: Verses 1–6 — "Released from the Law Through Death"

heb.paragraph: The verb katargeō in v. 2 describes the wife’s release from the marriage law when her husband dies. Paul applies this to the believer’s relationship to the law: through death with Christ (cf. 6:3–4), the believer has been ‘released’ from the law’s binding authority, not because the law died but because the believer died. The marriage analogy is slightly awkward in its application, which has generated extensive scholarly discussion.

refs.note: Paul declares: ‘through the law I died to the law so that I might live for God’ — the same death-to-law principle.
The letter kills but the Spirit gives life — the gramma/pneuma contrast Paul introduces in v. 6.

notes.note: The marriage analogy demonstrates that death dissolves legal obligations. In the spiritual realm, death with Christ dissolves the believer’s obligation to the law as a system of justification.
Believers died to the law ‘through the body of Christ’ — a reference to Christ’s crucifixion. The purpose: to belong to the risen Christ and ‘bear fruit for God.’
The ‘old way of the written code’ refers to the law’s external demands without the Spirit’s enabling power. The ‘new way of the Spirit’ is life under grace, empowered by the indwelling Holy Spirit.

Calvin notes that death to the law does not mean the law is abolished but that its condemning power has been terminated for the believer. The moral content of the law remains; its condemnation does not. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The phrase ‘the law of the husband’ (tou andros nomos) refers to the legal principle that binds a wife to her husband. The genitive is objective: the law concerning the husband.
The contrast between ‘the old way of the written code’ and ‘the new way of the Spirit’ anticipates 8:2–4, where the Spirit’s role is fully developed.

moo.source: Douglas J. Moo, Romans, NICNT (1996) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Moo acknowledges the imprecision of the marriage analogy (in the analogy the husband dies; in the application it is the wife/believer who dies) but argues the theological point is clear: death severs legal bonds, and believers have died to the law through Christ.

schreiner.source: Thomas R. Schreiner, Romans, BECNT (1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Schreiner emphasises that ‘bearing fruit for God’ is the purpose of release from the law. Freedom from law is not for autonomy but for fruitfulness. The law could command but not empower; the Spirit empowers what the law commanded. Schreiner's exegesis emphasizes the eschatological dimension of the passage. The author writes with the end in view — present suffering and ethical obligation are framed by the certainty of Christ's return and the final vindication of God's people. This already-not-yet tension shapes every imperative in the text.

hist.context: Romans 7:1–6 uses a marriage analogy to illustrate the believer’s new relationship to the law. Just as a married woman is bound to her husband while he lives but free to remarry after his death, so believers have ‘died to the law’ through Christ’s body in order to ‘belong to another’ — the risen Christ. The key pastoral implication is in v. 6: the old way of the ‘written code’ (gramma) gives way to the new way of the Spirit (pneuma). This prepares for the Spirit-centred theology of chapter 8.

heb.paragraph: Paul cites the tenth commandment (‘you shall not covet’) as the specific command that exposed his sinfulness. Epithymia is the most inward of sins: it cannot be externally observed or legislated against. The law’s prohibition of desire reveals that sin operates at the level of the heart, not merely in outward actions. This is why law alone cannot cure sin — it diagnoses the disease but cannot heal it.

Aphormē in v. 8 is a military term: a staging ground or base of operations from which an attack is launched. Sin uses the commandment as its aphormē, twisting the good command into an occasion for greater rebellion. The law is not sinful (v. 7), but sin is parasitic: it co-opts the law’s holiness for its own destructive purposes.

refs.note: The tenth commandment against coveting — the specific law Paul cites as exposing sin’s inward nature.
The serpent ‘deceived’ Eve — the same verb Paul uses for sin’s deceptive use of the commandment.
The law is good if used properly — Paul’s own later affirmation of the law’s goodness.

notes.note: The law is not sin, but it reveals sin. Without the command ‘do not covet,’ Paul would not have known the depth of his own sinful desires.

Sin, seizing its ‘opportunity’ (aphormē) through the commandment, produced every kind of covetous desire. The law’s prohibition paradoxically stimulates the very sin it forbids — not because the law is faulty, but because the human heart is corrupt.
The command intended to bring life brought death. The law promised life for obedience (Lev 18:5) but delivered death because of sin’s deceit. Sin is the villain, not the law.
Paul’s emphatic vindication of the law: it is holy, righteous, and good. Sin’s exceeding sinfulness is revealed precisely through its abuse of the good law.

Calvin argues that Paul speaks in his own person but as representative of the unregenerate under the law. The law’s prohibition arouses sin not by its own nature but because of the corruption of the human heart.

Calvin affirms the law’s inherent goodness. The fault lies entirely with sin, not with the law. This is crucial: Reformed theology does not denigrate the law but distinguishes between the law’s function and humanity’s inability to fulfil it.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The word aphormē was used in military contexts for a staging area or supply base. Sin is personified as a military adversary that commandeers the law’s holy command as its base of operations.
The verb exapatao (‘to deceive completely’) is used in Gen 3:13 LXX of the serpent’s deception of Eve. Paul may be intentionally recapitulating the Eden narrative.

moo.source: Douglas J. Moo, Romans, NICNT (1996) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Moo reads the ‘I’ as Paul speaking in the persona of Israel (and, behind Israel, Adam) under the law. The Eden echoes are deliberate: the commandment came, sin seized its opportunity, deception occurred, and death resulted. Paul is retelling Genesis 3 through the lens of Sinai.

schreiner.source: Thomas R. Schreiner, Romans, BECNT (1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Schreiner takes the ‘I’ as autobiographical but representative: Paul’s experience under the law typifies Israel’s corporate experience. The law exposed sin but could not restrain it. This is not a defect in the law but in the sinful nature that exploits it.

hist.context: Romans 7:7–13 defends the law against the implication that it is evil. Paul’s mē genoito (‘by no means!’ v. 7) is emphatic: the law is not sin. The problem is not the law but sin, which exploits the law’s good commands to produce greater transgression. Paul’s language echoes the Eden narrative: sin ‘deceived’ (exēpatēsen, v. 11) and ‘killed’ (apekteinen), just as the serpent ‘deceived’ Eve (Gen 3:13 LXX uses the same verb). Whether Paul is speaking autobiographically about his pre-conversion experience or using ‘I’ as a rhetorical device representing Israel/Adam is vigorously debated.

sections.header: Verses 14–25 — "What I Want to Do I Do Not Do"

heb.paragraph: Sarx (flesh) in v. 14 (‘I am of the flesh, sold under sin’) does not refer to the physical body per se but to the whole person as dominated by the power of sin. In Paul’s usage, sarx designates the unredeemed human condition in its weakness and hostility toward God. The contrast with pneuma (Spirit) that dominates chapter 8 is anticipated here: the flesh cannot do what the Spirit empowers.

The cry talaipōros egō anthrōpos (‘wretched man that I am!’ v. 24) is the rhetorical climax of the chapter. It expresses the anguish of one who recognises the good but cannot do it, who delights in God’s law inwardly but finds another law warring in the members. The cry is immediately answered in v. 25a (‘Thanks be to God, through Jesus Christ our Lord!’) and fully resolved in chapter 8.

refs.note: The flesh desires what is contrary to the Spirit and the Spirit what is contrary to the flesh — the same internal conflict Paul describes.
Paul acknowledges his own incompleteness and presses on toward the goal — the regenerate person’s ongoing struggle.
If we claim to be without sin, we deceive ourselves — the continuing reality of sin in the believer’s life.

notes.note: The shift to the present tense is significant. Paul now speaks of a current, ongoing experience. The regenerate person is spiritual but still has the body of flesh that is susceptible to sin’s pull.

The internal conflict: ‘what I want to do I do not do, but what I hate I do.’ This is the experience of the believer who delights in God’s law but finds the flesh resistant. It is not the experience of the unregenerate, who does not delight in God’s law at all (cf. 8:7–8).
The cry of the wretched man is not despair but longing for final redemption — the ‘redemption of our bodies’ mentioned in 8:23.
The answer to the cry: ‘Thanks be to God — through Jesus Christ our Lord!’ The resolution is not self-effort but Christ, whose work is applied by the Spirit in ch. 8.

Calvin reads the entire passage as describing the regenerate Christian. The delight in God’s law (v. 22) is impossible for the unregenerate, who is hostile to God (8:7). The struggle described here is the painful but real experience of the saint who is simultaneously justified and sinful (simul iustus et peccator).

Calvin sees the cry as expressing the believer’s groan for final deliverance, answered immediately by thanksgiving. The Christian life is this tension: already delivered in Christ, not yet delivered from the body of death.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The adjective sarkinos (‘fleshly,’ ‘made of flesh’) describes the human condition as still physically embodied and therefore vulnerable to sin, even after regeneration. Some MSS read sarkikos (‘characterized by flesh’), which would carry a stronger negative connotation.
The ‘law of sin’ (nomos tēs hamartias) is not the Mosaic law but a metaphorical ‘law’ or principle operating in the members. Paul uses nomos in a transferred sense: the pattern or power of sin.

moo.source: Douglas J. Moo, Romans, NICNT (1996) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Moo favours the regenerate interpretation, arguing that the present tense, the delight in God’s law (v. 22), and the ‘inner being’ (v. 22) are most naturally read as describing a believer’s experience. The unregenerate person does not ‘delight in God’s law’ (cf. 8:7). However, Moo notes that this is the believer viewed apart from the Spirit’s empowering — hence the contrast with chapter 8.
Moo reads the ‘body of death’ as the body insofar as it is still subject to sin and mortality. The cry is eschatological longing, and the answer is Christ, whose Spirit is the subject of chapter 8.

schreiner.source: Thomas R. Schreiner, Romans, BECNT (1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Schreiner reads the ‘I’ as the unregenerate person, or more precisely the person viewed under the law apart from the Spirit. The contrast with chapter 8 is decisive: chapter 7 describes the inability of the law to produce obedience; chapter 8 describes the Spirit’s power to accomplish what the law could not.

Schreiner argues that the ‘wretched man’ cry represents the culmination of life under the law’s condemnation. The answer (‘through Jesus Christ’) points forward to the liberating work of the Spirit in 8:1–4.

hist.context: Romans 7:14–25 is one of the most debated passages in the history of Christian theology. The central question is whether the ‘I’ describes Paul’s pre-conversion or post-conversion experience. Augustine (later in life), Luther, Calvin, and most Reformed interpreters read it as the ongoing struggle of the regenerate Christian with indwelling sin. The Greek Fathers (Origen, Chrysostom), Wesley, and many modern scholars read it as the experience of the unregenerate person or of Israel under the law. The shift to present tense (from the past tense of vv. 7–13) is the strongest argument for the regenerate reading. The passage’s theological contribution is clear regardless of the identity of ‘I’: the law alone cannot produce the obedience it demands. The resolution comes only through Christ (v. 25a) and the Spirit (8:1–4).

themes.note: Holiness at 8 reflects the intense focus on the believer’s struggle with sin and the law’s holy character. Justice at 7 reflects the forensic and moral dimensions of the argument.

rows.label: vv.1–6
rows.text: The Marriage Analogy: Released from the Law

rows.label: vv.7–13
rows.text: The Law and Sin: Good Command, Deadly Exploitation

rows.label: vv.14–20
rows.text: The Internal Conflict: Willing but Unable

rows.label: vv.21–25
rows.text: The Wretched Man and the Answer

lit.note: Romans 7 vindicates the law while exposing the powerlessness of law apart from the Spirit. The chapter’s agonised ‘I’ cries out for the resolution that comes in chapter 8.

hebtext.note: The verb katargeō in v. 2 describes the wife’s release from the marriage law when her husband dies. Paul applies this to the believer’s relationship to the law: through death with Christ (cf. 6:3–4), the believer has been ‘released’ from the law’s binding authority, not because the law died but because the believer died. The marriage analogy is slightly awkward in its application, which has generated extensive scholarly discussion.

Epithymia is the most inward of sins: it cannot be externally observed or legislated against. The law is not sinful (v. 7), but sin is parasitic: it co-opts the law’s holiness for its own destructive purposes.

ppl.name: Paul
ppl.role: Apostle to the Gentiles; author of thirteen NT letters
ppl.text: Paul (Saul of Tarsus) is introduced as a persecutor at Stephen’s stoning (Acts 7:58) and is dramatically converted on the Damascus road (Acts 9). He dominates Acts 13–28 across three missionary journeys, planting churches from Cyprus to Greece to Rome. His final journey to Rome as a prisoner frames Acts’ theological climax: the gospel reaching the empire’s capital, "boldly and without hindrance" (Acts 28:31).

ppl.name: Adam
ppl.text: Whether Paul is speaking autobiographically about his pre-conversion experience or using ‘I’ as a rhetorical device representing Israel/Adam is vigorously debated…

ppl.name: Christ
ppl.text: Just as a married woman is bound to her husband while he lives but free to remarry after his death, so believers have ‘died to the law’ through Christ’s body in order to ‘belong to another’ — the risen Christ…

trans.title: Comparing translations in The Law and Sin

translations.text: For example, by law a married woman is bound to her husband as long as he is alive, but if her husband dies, she is released from the law that binds her to him.
For a married woman is bound by law to her husband while he lives, but if her husband dies she is released from the law of marriage.
Do you not know, brothers and sisters—for I am speaking to those who know the law—that the law has authority over someone only as long as that person lives?
Or do you not know, brothers—for I am speaking to those who know the law—that the law is binding on a person only as long as he lives?

rec.title: John MacArthur
rec.quote: "The marriage analogy demonstrates that death dissolves legal obligations. In the spiritual realm, death with Christ dissolves the believer’s obligation to the law as a system of justification."

rec.title: John Calvin
rec.quote: "Calvin notes that death to the law does not mean the law is abolished but that its condemning power has been terminated for the believer. The moral content of the law remains; its condemnation does no…"

rec.title: NET Bible translators
rec.quote: "The phrase ‘the law of the husband’ (tou andros nomos) refers to the legal principle that binds a wife to her husband. The genitive is objective: the law concerning the husband."

thread.text: Paul declares: ‘through the law I died to the law so that I might live for God’ — the same death-to-law principle.
The letter kills but the Spirit gives life — the gramma/pneuma contrast Paul introduces in v. 6.
The tenth commandment against coveting — the specific law Paul cites as exposing sin’s inward nature.
The serpent ‘deceived’ Eve — the same verb Paul uses for sin’s deceptive use of the commandment.
The law is good if used properly — Paul’s own later affirmation of the law’s goodness.
The flesh desires what is contrary to the Spirit and the Spirit what is contrary to the flesh — the same internal conflict Paul describes.

discourse.thesis: We have died to the law through Christ's body; though the law is holy, sin uses it to produce death — the flesh wages war against the inner being.

nodes.text: The law binds a person only while alive — a married woman is bound to her husband while he lives, but released by his death. The marriage analogy: death severs the legal bond.

You also died to the law through the body of Christ, so that you might belong to another — to him raised from the dead — to bear fruit for God. When controlled by the flesh, sinful passions aroused by the law bore fruit for death. But now, released from the law, we serve in the new way of the Spirit.
Is the law sin? By no means! Yet I would not have known sin except through the law. I would not have known covetousness if the law had not said, 'You shall not covet.'

Sin, seizing opportunity through the commandment, produced in me every kind of covetousness. Apart from the law, sin lies dead. I was once alive apart from the law, but when the commandment came, sin sprang to life and I died. The very commandment meant for life brought death. Sin deceived me and killed me through the commandment. The law is holy; the commandment is holy, righteous, and good.

Did what is good bring death? By no means! Sin, that it might be shown to be sin, produced death through what is good — sin through the commandment became utterly sinful. We know the law is spiritual, but I am of the flesh, sold under sin.

I do not understand my own actions. I do not do what I want, but the very thing I hate. If I do what I do not want, I agree the law is good. It is no longer I who do it, but sin dwelling in me. Nothing good dwells in my flesh — I have the desire to do what is right, but not the ability. The evil I do not want is what I keep doing.

children.text: If I do what I do not want, it is no longer I who do it, but sin that dwells within me.

nodes.text: I find this law at work: when I want to do good, evil is right there with me. In my inner being I delight in God's law, but I see another law waging war against the law of my mind and making me a prisoner of the law of sin. Wretched man that I am! Who will deliver me from this body of death? Thanks be to God through Jesus Christ our Lord!

discourse.note: The 'I' of Romans 7:14-25 is debated: is Paul describing pre-conversion experience, Christian struggle, or humanity under law generally? The 'wretched man' cry (v. 24) sets up the triumphant answer of chapter 8.

coaching.tip: 'I do not do what I want, but I do the very thing I hate' (v. 15). Is this pre-conversion Paul or ongoing Christian struggle? Scholars divide. Either way, the passage exposes the limits of moral effort: knowing the good doesn't empower doing the good. The law diagnoses but doesn't cure.

'Wretched man that I am! Who will deliver me?' (v. 24). The cry for rescue admits defeat. Self-reformation failed. But immediately: 'Thanks be to God through Jesus Christ our Lord!' (v. 25). The answer isn't better effort but a different deliverer. Chapter 8's Spirit-power answers chapter 7's flesh-failure.

prayer_prompt: As Paul cries 'What I want to do I do not do, but what I hate I do,' consider asking God to meet you in the honest frustration of your own moral failure — and to remind you of the rescue that follows.
